does: This paper develops Jailbreak Mimicry, an automated system that generates narrative-based adversarial prompts to bypass safety mechanisms in large language models. The core contribution is training a compact attacker model (Mistral-7B with LoRA fine-tuning) on curated jailbreak examples from AdvBench to produce one-shot jailbreak prompts in a reproducible manner. The methodology achieves 81.0% attack success rate against GPT-OSS-20B on a held-out test set of 200 items, substantially outperforming manual prompt engineering. Cross-model evaluation demonstrates variable vulnerability patterns: 66.5% success against GPT-4, 79.5% against Llama-3, and 33.0% against Gemini 2.5 Flash. The work transforms adversarial prompt discovery from ad-hoc craftsmanship into a systematic, scalable process suitable for proactive security assessment in AI systems.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of adversarial attacks against LLM safety mechanisms, extending prior research on prompt injection and jailbreaking vulnerabilities documented in projects like AdvBench and the GPT Red-Teaming Challenge. It advances beyond manual jailbreak discovery by incorporating automated prompt generation techniques inspired by adversarial machine learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA. The paper responds to recognized limitations in existing red-teaming approaches—which rely on human ingenuity and are difficult to reproduce—by applying meta-learning concepts where smaller models learn to generate adversarial inputs. It complements concurrent work on understanding contextual vulnerabilities and narrative-based exploits in LLMs, positioning automated discovery as an alternative to labor-intensive manual assessments.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for scalable, automated vulnerability discovery that could accelerate red-teaming workflows and proactive security auditing of LLMs. The approach suggests potential follow-up research into transferability of generated jailbreaks across model families, optimization of attacker model architectures, and integration with defensive mechanisms that learn from adversarial patterns. The significant variation in cross-model vulnerability patterns (33% to 81% ASR) points toward developing model-specific defenses and understanding which architectural or training choices confer robustness. Future directions could explore ensemble attacks, adversarial training loops where defenses adapt to generated jailbreaks, and deeper mechanistic understanding of why narrative framing exploits safety mechanisms. The methodology also enables systematic benchmarking of safety mechanisms and could support development of more resilient safeguards in next-generation LLMs.
